ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Bee Hoon Kwah; mapped to generic noodle dish class as closest defensible ancestor. Bee Hoon Kwah is a hybrid dish combining stir-fried rice vermicelli with a crispy dosa-style rice-flour pancake.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (rice noodle dish, stir-fried) used as closest defensible match. Bee Hoon Kwah includes a pan-fried rice-flour pancake component not captured by this code alone.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical food concept identified for this specific dish. Not clinically coded as a distinct entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Bee Hoon Kwah weighs approximately 400 g, comprising a crispy pan-fried rice-flour pancake served alongside stir-fried rice vermicelli with vegetables and spices.

nutrient_source.title: Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database — Stir-fried Bee Hoon (adapted) and USDA FoodData Central — Rice flour pancake / dosa analogue
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board, Singapore; United States Department of Agriculture
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Bee Hoon Kwah as a combined dish. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore stir-fried bee hoon (rice vermicelli) data and USDA FoodData Central dosa/rice-flour pancake analogue, weighted approximately 60% bee hoon component and 40% kwah (pancake) component. The input record values (energy 137.5 kcal, protein 3.0 g, fat 5.0 g, carbohydrate 18.75 g per 100 g) are consistent with this blended profile and are retained with medium confidence. Cholesterol is reported as 0.0 mg/100 g, consistent with a fully plant-based preparation; this is confirmed zero per the vegan ingredient profile.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database — Rice vermicelli and rice-flour pancake analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bee Hoon Kwah as a combined dish. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on: (1) rice vermicelli GI ~58–61 per University of Sydney GI Database (white rice vermicelli, boiled); (2) plain dosa / rice-flour pancake GI ~69 per Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables; weighted blend ~60% vermicelli / 40% pancake yields estimated composite GI ≈ 63–65. Lentil content in the kwah batter moderates GI slightly. GL per 400 g serving = (65 × 18.75 × 4) / 100 = 48.75 g. Classified as medium GI (56–69 range).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 300 mg/100 g; a 400 g plate delivers approximately 1,200 mg sodium, representing 52–60% of the recommended daily limit of 2,000–2,300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced salt during preparation and limit portion size to 200–250 g per meal, pairing with potassium-rich low-sodium foods such as leafy vegetables to partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Estimated GI of 65 (medium) and a glycemic load of approximately 48.75 g per 400 g plate; rice vermicelli and rice flour are moderate-to-high GI carbohydrate sources.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200 g, ensure the lentil-containing kwah batter is included to moderate the glycaemic response, and pair with non-starchy vegetables to reduce the overall glycaemic load of the meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is low at 1.25 g/100 g, cholesterol is 0 mg/100 g, and the dish is fully plant-based; however, sodium at 300 mg/100 g warrants attention for patients with heart failure or sodium-sensitive CVD.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Bee Hoon Kwah is a low-saturated-fat, cholesterol-free dish suitable for most cardiovascular disease patients when prepared with minimal oil and reduced salt; patients with heart failure or sodium-restricted diets should limit portion size to 200 g per meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices including turmeric (curcumin), cumin, coriander, mustard seeds, and curry leaves; low saturated fat and zero cholesterol.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The anti-inflammatory spice profile of Bee Hoon Kwah may be beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis patients; however, green chillies are nightshades and should be monitored for individual joint sensitivity, and oil used in pan-frying should be minimised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bee Hoon Kwah is a distinctive Singaporean and Malaysian dish that combines two preparations: stir-fried rice vermicelli (bee hoon) seasoned with vegetables and aromatic spices, served alongside a crispy pan-fried rice-flour pancake (kwah) that is reminiscent of a South Indian dosa. The kwah component is typically made from a batter of rice flour and lentils (such as urad dal), flavoured with turmeric, cumin, coriander, mustard seeds, curry leaves, and green chillies, then pan-fried until golden and crispy on the outside while remaining slightly soft within. The bee hoon component is stir-fried with vegetables and the same spice palette, creating a cohesive flavour profile across both elements. The dish is fully plant-based, halal, vegetarian, and vegan, making it accessible across multiple dietary communities in Singapore and Malaysia. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 137.5 kcal, 3.0 g protein, 5.0 g fat (of which 1.25 g is saturated), and 18.75 g carbohydrate with 1.5 g dietary fibre and only 1.0 g sugar. Sodium is 300 mg/100 g, which is borderline for hypertension management. The estimated glycaemic index is 65 (medium), and the glycaemic load per standard 400 g plate is approximately 48.75 g, making portion control important for Type 2 Diabetes management. The anti-inflammatory spice profile — particularly curcumin from turmeric — may offer benefits for inflammatory conditions. The dish is culturally rooted in the South Indian culinary influence on Singaporean and Malaysian hawker food culture.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Bee Hoon Kwah as a combined dish. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore stir-fried bee hoon data and USDA FoodData Central dosa/rice-flour pancake analogues, weighted by estimated component proportions. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed international GI tables. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level data from approved sources is robust and the blended values are internally consistent.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific combined dish (Bee Hoon Kwah) are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a single composite entry as of 2024. 2. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 mg/100 g: confirmed zero — the dish is fully plant-based with no animal-derived ingredients; this is consistent with the vegan dietary flag and confirmed by the ingredient profile. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.03 g/100 g: trace level, below meaningful clinical threshold; retained from input record consistent with small amounts from cooking oil. 4. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore stir-fried bee hoon and USDA FoodData Central dosa analogue; see nutrient_source for full adaptation methodology. 5. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Bee Hoon Kwah exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. The input record cited foodo.sg as the HPB source URL; the canonical HPB Foodo URL is https://foodo.hpb.gov.sg/ and has been used in this record. 7. FoodEx2 code A0CKP is used as the closest defensible approximate match; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for this hybrid dish.
